page: Google Cloud Platform c3d-highmem-8-lssd is a memory-optimized virtual machine instance featuring 8 dedicated vCPUs powered by an AMD EPYC 9B14 processor with an x86_64 architecture. The hardware configuration includes 4 physical cores with 2 threads per core, a 32 MB L3 cache, and 64 GB of system memory, yielding a high memory-to-vCPU ratio of 8 GB per core. Additionally, the instance comes equipped with 402 GB of local NVMe SSD storage, while containing no GPU accelerators.

Performance benchmarks indicate strong single-core CPU capabilities, with stress-ng single-core performance and Geekbench text processing scoring in the top 25%. Multi-core CPU performance, Passmark CPU scores, and static web serving throughput remain in the average tier. Memory read bandwidth is average for both small and large blocks, but large-block write memory bandwidth is a notable bottleneck, scoring in the bottom 10%. LLM inference speeds are average for small 135M models, but prompt processing for medium 7B models drops to the bottom 25%.

From a qualitative cost-efficiency perspective, the high memory density combined with integrated local NVMe SSD storage provides a balanced profile for memory-intensive workloads without the overhead of external storage attachments. This configuration is well-suited for memory-bound applications, database operations, text processing, and caching layers like Redis, though it is less optimal for write-heavy memory operations or large-scale LLM inference.
description: Google Cloud Platform c3d-highmem-8-lssd is a memory-optimized instance featuring 8 dedicated vCPUs from an AMD EPYC 9B14 processor, 64 GB of RAM, and 402 GB of local NVMe SSD storage. Operating on the x86_64 architecture with 4 physical cores, it delivers strong single-core CPU and text processing performance, placing in the top 25% of benchmarks. However, multi-core execution and memory read bandwidth are average, while large-block memory write bandwidth is a performance bottleneck, ranking in the bottom 10%. The instance's high memory-to-vCPU ratio of 8 GB per core, combined with local high-speed storage, offers qualitative resource density for memory-intensive workloads. It is best suited for database operations, caching layers, and text processing tasks that require significant memory capacity but do not demand heavy memory write throughput.
